caveats: a: Four-digit occupation data include different nonferrous processes and product mixes.
a: Existing machine controls, optimization software, and automated inspection are not separated from not-yet-automated tasks.
rho: No six-digit longitudinal AI implementation study was located.
rho: Higher-specification plants may gain more from documentation but implement more slowly because validation burdens are greater.
e: Public data do not separate toll processing from metal-product sales.
e: The injected n uses a machinery margin proxy that may not reflect volatile metal pass-through revenue.
s5: Cross-industry intentions are not completed transactions.
s5: Strategic asset purchases and family transfers may not preserve an eligible standalone firm.
q: No public evidence directly measures productivity pass-through.
q: Reported revenue and margin can be distorted by large changes in metal prices.
d5: Four-digit output includes processes outside 331491.
d5: Demand and qualification cycles vary sharply across nickel, titanium, zinc, lead, precious, and refractory metal shapes.
o: This measures operator requirement, not employment intensity.
o: Material or geography substitution belongs primarily in d5.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is earned through conversion charges and per-pound or per-lot sales, commonly with metal-price pass-through, specifications, certifications, minimum lots, and repeat industrial purchase orders.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code mixes commodity mill-product manufacturing with outsourced toll and custom conversion. The lens retains repeat external processing relationships because treating captive and commodity-integrated mills as recurring service firms would be incoherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Copper and aluminum processors, captive mills, integrated primary smelters, businesses focused on speculative metal ownership rather than processing, project shells, and non-control investments.
lens.included_activities: Independent US toll, contract, and custom processors that repeatedly roll, draw, or extrude purchased nickel, titanium, zinc, lead, precious, refractory, or other nonferrous metals into customer-specified shapes and have transferable operations in the target band.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for broader NAICS 331400 rather than 331491. I apply AI exposure to quoting, specifications, quality records, production planning, procurement, inventory, maintenance knowledge, compliance, sales, and administration, while assigning little direct substitution to machine setup, furnace work, inspection, and material handling. The broader group includes smelting and refining, which may be more process-heavy than rolling and drawing, so it likely understates the target industry's exposure modestly.
caveats: Four-digit occupation data include different nonferrous processes and product mixes.
Existing machine controls, optimization software, and automated inspection are not separated from not-yet-automated tasks.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study across titanium, nickel, zinc, precious-metal, and specialty-wire processors, with current ERP/MES and automation use, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Broader nonferrous processing is 53.18% production employment and 7.66% installation, maintenance, and repair. Extruding and drawing operators alone are 10.47% of employment. Office support is 7.76%, and management, engineering, planning, quality, and technical sales add higher-wage document and decision workflows where AI can reduce effort or avoid hiring.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for broader NAICS 331400, not constant-quality demand for the narrowed 331491 repeat-processing basket. I translate the 2.4% annual 2024-34 broader output projection into a moderated five-year base because smelting, refining, alloying, and commodity price/grade mix differ. Critical-material uses support the upside, but import competition and customer substitution can make the broader projection overstate domestic processing demand.
caveats: Four-digit output includes processes outside 331491.
Demand and qualification cycles vary sharply across nickel, titanium, zinc, lead, precious, and refractory metal shapes.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year customer forecasts and backlog by alloy/end market, constant-volume shipment histories, announced domestic capacity, import penetration, and aerospace/energy program ramps would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: BLS projects broader nonferrous-ex-aluminum output from 50.3 to 64.1 billion chained dollars through 2034. DOE identifies nickel, titanium, cobalt, zinc, refractory and precious metals across batteries, aerospace, defense, high-performance alloys, catalysts, electronics, and energy systems. Those end markets support growth, tempered by cyclicality and import dependence.

caveats: Public data do not separate toll processing from metal-product sales.
The injected n uses a machinery margin proxy that may not reflect volatile metal pass-through revenue.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of toll versus metal-sale revenue, repeat-customer share, ownership, captive status, normalized EBITDA, certifications, and management independence would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Custom dimensions, alloys, certifications, minimum lots, and toll-conversion arrangements can create repeat outsourced relationships. Eligibility is reduced by captive facilities, integrated scrap recovery, commodity-oriented mills, customer concentration, metal working-capital exposure, and firms lacking standalone management. The injected firm count is estimated from size classes rather than observed normalized EBITDA.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 331491 ($1,321,231,754) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 331491 ($6,842,264,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $522,310 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 331491); class <5: 57 firms, avg revenue/firm $840,140 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $164,836 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,212,714 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $434,135 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,298,040 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,235,675 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $19,978,786 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,919,838 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $48,422,692 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,500,532 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $222,015,870 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $43,559,514 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 80

caveats: This measures operator requirement, not employment intensity.
Material or geography substitution belongs primarily in d5.
o.change_evidence: Customer insourcing plans, distributed autonomous fabrication, alternate-process qualification, and supplier-consolidation data would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Customers require physical, certified shapes produced through rolling, drawing, extrusion, heat treatment, finishing, testing, and traceable release. Software can simplify ordering and engineering exchange but cannot replace the accountable processor. Limited downside reflects customer insourcing or qualification of alternate mills rather than software self-service.

caveats: No public evidence directly measures productivity pass-through.
Reported revenue and margin can be distorted by large changes in metal prices.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, conversion-charge formulas, index clauses, bid histories, price-reset cadence, and contribution margins net of metal value would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Metal-price pass-through and conversion-charge pricing can isolate process economics, allowing some labor benefit to be retained between repricings. Sophisticated aerospace, industrial, electronics, and distribution buyers rebid qualified sources and negotiate conversion charges, so savings should be shared over five years. Tight qualifications and scarce alloy capabilities support higher retention than pure commodity metal sales.

caveats: No six-digit longitudinal AI implementation study was located.
Higher-specification plants may gain more from documentation but implement more slowly because validation burdens are greater.
rho.change_evidence: Plant pilots measuring accepted quotes, planner hours, schedule adherence, scrap, rework, certificate errors, maintenance downtime, and customer escapes would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: AI-assisted quoting, specification review, scheduling, quality-document preparation, maintenance retrieval, purchasing, and customer service can be implemented within five years. Realization is constrained by aerospace, medical, defense, and industrial certifications, heat and lot traceability, legacy MES/ERP integration, cybersecurity, small data sets, and accountable human release of product.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures planned five-year sales or transfers among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed control transfers in specialty nonferrous processing. I anchor near its 22% employer intention rate but discount for customer qualifications, environmental exposure, working capital, and owner-dependent technical relationships that can prevent a qualifying close. Intentions likely overstate completed transfers.
caveats: Cross-industry intentions are not completed transactions.
Strategic asset purchases and family transfers may not preserve an eligible standalone firm.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age data and five years of closed transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, SBA lending, trade advisers, and environmental permit ownership changes would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: General succession pressure is material: Gallup reports 52.3% of employer businesses have owners age 55 or older and 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years. Specialty processors are operationally transferable, but qualification and capital requirements narrow the realized pool.

principal_driver: Qualification-heavy repeat processing relationships can retain workflow gains while critical-material end markets expand.
principal_weakness: More than half of employment is physical production, and public data cannot isolate toll-processing firms or six-digit task exposure.

run_meta.title: Nonferrous Metal (except Copper and Aluminum) Rolling, Drawing, and Extruding
